claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

- Is your child **under 16 years old**?
- Is your child **between 16 and 19 years old**?
- Is your child **20 years old or over**?

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Alex is 8 years old, which means he's under 16 and automatically qualifies based on age. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Since Alex is only 8 years old, this question about working hours would typically apply to older children. However, to confirm:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in any paid work at all)?**

At 8 years old, I'd expect the answer is yes - he's not working - but I just want to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not working at all. He's just a kid - he goes to school like any other 8-year-old!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That makes perfect sense! 

Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in the child's own name. For an 8-year-old, this would almost certainly be "no" - he wouldn't be receiving these benefits himself. Can you confirm Alex isn't receiving any such benefits in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name. He's just 8 - I don't think that's even possible at his age!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're absolutely right - these questions are more relevant to older teenagers! 

**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

Again, at 8 years old, I'm sure the answer is no - he's in primary school. Just confirming so we can move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! He's in primary school, not doing any apprenticeship. He's only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Of course! Now we're moving on to questions about you as the claimant.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK. Does that apply to you, or do you meet any of the other criteria?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester, so no issues there with residency.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, Manchester counts perfectly! You meet the residency requirement.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex** - this is the most common situation
2. **You contribute to his upkeep** - paying at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that covers his upkeep

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that's straightforward!

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This could be:
- **No one else** is claiming for him
- **Someone else was claiming**, but you've both agreed that you should now be the one to claim
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim (e.g., between separated parents)

Which situation applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming for him. It's just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, that makes things simple!

**Where is Alex currently living?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:

- Is Alex **in hospital**?
- Is Alex **in local authority care**?
- Is Alex **being fostered**?
- Or is Alex **living at home normally** with you?

I'm guessing from what you've told me that he's living at home normally, but just want to confirm!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, Alex has been in hospital for a while now. He's been there for a few months dealing with a medical issue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Alex has been in hospital. I hope he's getting the care he needs and recovers well.

Let me check the rules around hospital stays.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I need to ask a bit more about Alex's hospital stay.

**How long has Alex been in hospital?**

The rules around hospital stays are:
- Child Benefit normally continues for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- There's also a **linking rule**: if a child returns to hospital within 28 days of leaving, the time from both stays is added together towards the 12-week limit

Has Alex been in hospital for:
- **Less than 12 weeks** (cumulative), or
- **More than 12 weeks** (cumulative)?

Also, has he had multiple hospital stays, or has this been one continuous stay?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been there for about 15 weeks now - it's been one continuous stay, so it's definitely over the 12-week mark.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks, which is over the 12-week limit, we need to check one more thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - there's an exception to the 12-week rule!

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Bringing him clothes, toys, books, or other items
- Buying him snacks or treats
- Paying for anything else for his care or comfort

If you (or your spouse/civil partner if you live together) are regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay, you may still be eligible for Child Benefit even though he's been there for more than 12 weeks.

Are you spending money on him regularly?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy him clothes, magazines, and his favorite snacks when I visit. I try to make sure he has what he needs while he's in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's lovely that you're supporting Alex that way. That spending counts as regularly contributing to his care, which means the exception applies!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, we're nearly there! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's hospital stay being funded through the NHS (as normal), rather than the local council paying specifically for his accommodation or care costs?

If the local council is paying for his accommodation or maintenance, that would affect eligibility. But if it's just standard NHS hospital care, then you should be fine.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything. It's just regular NHS hospital care - nothing to do with the local council.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! That's the final check complete.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

I'm pleased to confirm that you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### Summary of Your Situation:
- ✅ Alex is under 16 (age 8)
- ✅ You live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✅ You are responsible for Alex (he lives with you)
- ✅ No one else is claiming for him
- ✅ **Hospital stay exception applies** - Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (over the usual 12-week limit), you qualify because you're regularly spending money on him (clothes, magazines, snacks)
- ✅ The local council is not paying for his care

### Next Steps to Claim:
1. **Apply online** or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. Payments are usually made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note:
If you or a partner earn over **£60,000** per year, be aware of the **High Income Child Benefit Charge** - this may affect how much you keep, but it doesn't affect your eligibility to claim.

For more information on how to claim, visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers soon! Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 and automatically qualifies based on age; (2) He is not working; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) His parent lives in the UK (Manchester) meeting residency requirements; (6) His parent is responsible for him as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the normal 12-week limit), his parent regularly spends money on him during the hospital stay (clothes, magazines, snacks), which qualifies for the exception to the 12-week rule; (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance - it's standard NHS care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for over 12 weeks, the claimant qualifies for the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay. All other eligibility criteria are met: appropriate age, UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no council funding involved.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 and automatically qualifies based on age; (2) He is not working; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) His parent lives in the UK (Manchester) meeting residency requirements; (6) His parent is responsible for him as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the normal 12-week limit), his parent regularly spends money on him during the hospital stay (clothes, magazines, snacks), which qualifies for the exception to the 12-week rule; (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance - it's standard NHS care.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for over 12 weeks, the claimant qualifies for the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay. All other eligibility criteria are met: appropriate age, UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no council funding involved.
